title: What Schools Receive
text:
All participating schools receive subsidized access to Science-of-Reading–aligned literacy tutoring for students in grades 4–8. Tutoring is:

Delivered during the school day, with no after-school logistics required
Provided in small groups or one-to-one settings for personalized instruction
Delivered in sessions at least 90 minutes a week for at least ten weeks
Focused on the core skills the Science of Reading identifies as foundational: phonics, fluency, vocabulary, and comprehension
Delivered by experienced and vetted tutoring providers that partner with CSC

title: What Schools Receive
text:
All participating schools receive subsidized access to Science-of-Reading–aligned literacy tutoring for students in grades 4–8. Tutoring is:

Delivered during the school day, with no after-school logistics required
Provided in small groups or one-to-one settings for personalized instruction
Delivered in sessions at least 90 minutes a week for at least ten weeks
Focused on the core skills the Science of Reading identifies as foundational: phonics, fluency, vocabulary, and comprehension
Delivered by experienced and vetted tutoring providers that partner with CSC

title: What Schools Receive
text:
Implementation Support
In either SY 2026–27 or SY 2027–28, schools will receive:

Structured Planning Support via Design Sprints
Design Sprints consist of four half-day sessions, delivered once a week across four weeks. During these sprints, CSC works with your school team to build tutoring into your existing schedule, identifying the right time blocks, student groups, and logistics before tutoring begins. They are cohort-based and aim to help school teams shift mindsets and build technical skills to launch and support higher-quality, higher-impact tutoring interventions. Design Sprints will likely be in person, but could be virtual or hybrid depending on LEA preference. Design sprint topics include:

Session 1: Grounding in Equity and Aligning on Tutoring
Session 2: Clarifying the Problem Through Data
Session 3: Gathering Empathy and Insight
Session 4: Designing for Impact



Ongoing Coaching and Communities of Practice
CSC coaches check in regularly with school points of contact and tutoring providers to troubleshoot challenges and ensure quality. Schools will also connect with other schools during Community of Practice check-ins to offer peer support and brainstorming.


Quality Monitoring
CSC observes tutoring at least twice a year using its HIT Evaluation Tool to assess fidelity to the model and provides data tools for schools to track whether tutoring is happening consistently and at the right dosage.


Sustainability Planning
CSC will help schools create a long-term plan for integrating tutoring into their schedules and future budgets.

title: Sign Up to Learn More
text:
CSC and Bellwether will hold regular virtual information sessions for school and district leaders from May through August 2026. Attendees will be able to learn more about the program and the study, hear directly from initiative and study team members, and ask any questions they have. All schools and districts are asked to attend a session or speak one-on-one with CSC and Bellwether before committing to participation. Sign up to attend a session below:
Sign Up for an Info Session
We hope to see you soon!

title: CitySchools Collaborative is partnering with schools to provide science-of-reading–aligned literacy tutoring to students in grades 4–8 and support for school administrators and SEAs. They’re looking for schools ready to make a difference for their readers.
text: Sign Up for an Info Session Read More About the Study

title: CitySchools Collaborative is partnering with schools to provide science-of-reading–aligned literacy tutoring to students in grades 4–8 and support for school administrators and SEAs. They’re looking for schools ready to make a difference for their readers.
text:
The Opportunity
Recent research has shown that high-impact tutoring (HIT) can be highly effective at increasing students’ reading proficiency. However, tutoring’s effectiveness depends on the availability of tutors, fidelity to the model, dosage, and instructional quality.
That’s where CitySchools Collaborative (CSC) comes in. CSC is leading a federally-funded initiative to bring structured, science-of-reading–aligned literacy tutoring directly into schools and to study which supports make that tutoring effective. Participating schools receive subsidies to bring high-impact tutoring to their students for up to two years, along with hands-on planning and implementation support from the CSC team.
This is an opportunity to improve your students’ literacy outcomes, build administrative capacity, and contribute to research that will inform how schools across the country support literacy tutoring.

title: CitySchools Collaborative is partnering with schools to provide science-of-reading–aligned literacy tutoring to students in grades 4–8 and support for school administrators and SEAs. They’re looking for schools ready to make a difference for their readers.
text:
Who This Is For
This opportunity is designed for school leaders in Maryland, D.C., and Virginia looking to strengthen literacy outcomes for students in grades 4–8.

title: CitySchools Collaborative is partnering with schools to provide science-of-reading–aligned literacy tutoring to students in grades 4–8 and support for school administrators and SEAs. They’re looking for schools ready to make a difference for their readers.
text:
What Schools Receive

Subsidized literacy tutoring delivered during the school day for at least one year. The cost to each school depends on the customized model designed with CSC’s expert support.
Custom design support from CSC staff to integrate tutoring into your schedule and ensure it runs smoothly.
Ongoing coaching and Communities of Practice to work through challenges as they emerge.
Data tools and quality monitoring to help you track student progress and ensure tutoring is consistent and effective.
Sustainability planning support to integrate tutoring into schools’ long-term models and financial plans.

title: CitySchools Collaborative is partnering with schools to provide science-of-reading–aligned literacy tutoring to students in grades 4–8 and support for school administrators and SEAs. They’re looking for schools ready to make a difference for their readers.
text:
CSC’s Impact
A previous study of CSC’s tutoring program found that students who received high-intensity math tutoring from CSC providers in 2023–2025 received the equivalent of an additional 59 instructional days and achieved, on average, 138% of their expected growth on math assessments.
→ Read the QED Report

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
CSC will provide subsidies that cover all or most of the cost of tutoring. The cost per student to schools depends on several factors, such as whether schools select a virtual or in-person model, whether they use small groups or 1-on-1 tutoring, and whether they choose to use existing school staff as tutors or bring in a provider. See the examples below to see how the typical costs might break down depending on these choices:






Virtual Tutoring Provider



School A engages with TFA Ignite. Students meet with college tutors virtually. A school staff member serves as a site leader to supervise students during sessions and liaise with TFA Ignite to manage the program. School A ensures students have working technology and designates a tutoring space. CSC funding subsidizes the cost of virtual tutors and provides a stipend for the TFA Ignite site leader, a School A teacher.
Required School Costs: ~$500/student for tutoring








In-Person Tutoring Provider



School B engages with Reading Assist. Reading Assist provides tutors and employs a program manager that supports tutors across a few schools and coordinates with school leadership. CSC funding to Reading Assist subsidizes the program cost including tutors, materials, and site management.
Required School Costs: ~$750/student for tutoring (receives full-time staff as tutors)








Staff-Led Model



School C uses their own staff to lead tutoring groups throughout the day as part of their regular responsibilities. School C has assigned an instructional coach to manage student assignments, track session-level student data, provide coaching, and manage reporting. CSC provides a stipend for the instructional coach managing the program; staff-tutors’ full salaries are included in the School C budget.
Required School Costs: ~$200–600/student for tutoring

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: Will this replace our literacy curriculum?
text:
Will this replace our literacy curriculum?
No. The tutoring program is specifically designed to complement and align with your school’s existing core literacy instruction, not replace it. CSC works with your team to ensure tutoring reinforces what students are learning in their regular classes.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: How much time does tutoring take?
text:
How much time does tutoring take?
Tutoring is approximately 90 minutes per week, delivered during the school day, for a minimum of 10 weeks. CSC works with school leaders to identify time blocks that fit your existing schedule. Tutoring can happen 1-on-1 or in small groups of up to four students.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
What grades are eligible?
The program serves students in grades 4–8.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
What schools are eligible?
All schools that serve at least one grade between grades 4 and 8 and are not special-purpose schools (e.g., schools for deaf students, schools in juvenile detention centers, language immersion schools, etc.) are eligible for the initiative.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: How are students selected for tutoring?
text:
How are students selected for tutoring?
Schools work with CSC to identify students who would benefit most from literacy support. CSC provides guidance on selection criteria based on student assessment data and teacher input.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
What about student data privacy?
Student privacy is taken seriously at every level of this project. All data sharing follows applicable federal privacy laws (including FERPA) and district/state agreements. Research findings are reported in aggregate only without identifying individual students or schools. The research is conducted by Bellwether, an independent organization, in accordance with federal research standards. Bellwether will use the following practices to ensure data security throughout the evaluation:

Bellwether shall designate Dr. Daniela Torre Gibney as the person responsible for the security and confidentiality of the data and will notify all study partners immediately in writing of any change in designee. Bellwether will use appropriate safeguards to prevent the use or disclosure of the information other than as provided by the data sharing agreement. Dr. Torre Gibney shall instruct staff with access to confidential information about the requirements for handling confidential information.
All Bellwether staff use password-protected company computers and are required to go through rigorous training in computer security and safety.
All survey data will be made unidentifiable before being sent to Bellwether.
Only Bellwether IT staff and the assigned researchers working on the project will have access to sensitive individual-level data, including student academic test scores, program data, interview/focus group notes, and interview/focus group recordings.
All study data will be stored in a secure, access-controlled Google Drive folder, accessible only to authorized Bellwether research personnel. Within the folder will be sub-folders for each participating school, which will be shared only with the corresponding school contact for each folder. Schools will be able to upload their data directly into their individual Google Drive folder. If any participating school has a preferred method of secure data transfer, Bellwether will set up those processes.
All program, survey, assessment, and other data will be shared via encrypted transfer if not uploaded directly to Google Drive. No data will be shared via email.
No study data will be stored on unencrypted laptop computers or other portable computing devices or media (e.g., flash drives).
Bellwether will comply with FERPA, applicable state privacy laws, and all data security provisions in the data sharing agreement made with each school.
All data will be destroyed or returned to each data sharing partner when the study is over, as laid out in the DSA or MOU with each partner.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
How long is the commitment?
Most schools offer tutoring for two academic years, depending on interest, capacity, and study design. CSC will discuss the specifics of the timeline with your school during the sign-up process. CSC’s hands-on support will last one academic year for each school.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: What if my school is assigned to the delayed treatment group?
text:
What if my school is assigned to the delayed treatment group?
All participating schools receive access to literacy tutoring in Year 1 (2026–27). Schools in the delayed implementation group receive CSC’s full planning and coaching support in Year 2 (2027–28). The delayed group still receives all the benefits of participation.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: Will this interfere with state testing or other assessments?
text:
Will this interfere with state testing or other assessments?
CSC works with your school team during the planning phase to build a tutoring schedule that accounts for testing calendars and other school priorities to minimize disruption and maximize consistency. No research activities will take place during your school’s state testing window.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: What if we need to pause or stop tutoring?
text:
What if we need to pause or stop tutoring?
CSC understands that schools face unexpected challenges. Your CSC contact will work with you to navigate any scheduling disruptions.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
Who conducts the tutoring?
Schools can select to train their existing support staff to serve as tutors or receive support from CSC to select one of its experienced, vetted tutoring providers to deliver tutoring directly to students. CSC staff oversee implementation quality and provide coaching and support. Your school designates a point of contact who works with CSC throughout the process.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: What is the Science of Reading?
text:
What is the Science of Reading?
The Science of Reading refers to a body of research spanning cognitive science, linguistics, and education that explains how people learn to read. It emphasizes explicit, systematic instruction in phonics, phonemic awareness, fluency, vocabulary, and comprehension. Tutoring in this program is aligned to these research-backed principles.
Learn more:

Defining the Science of Reading
In-depth guide to the Science of Reading
Stocktaking report on approaches to reading
Resources supported by the Maryland State Education Association

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
When will the study begin?
A kick-off meeting with all participating schools and CSC will take place virtually in July–August 2026.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: When will I know which tutoring provider my school will use?
text:
When will I know which tutoring provider my school will use?
School leaders should select their tutoring provider by the end of July to prepare for the school year. CSC will help connect schools to potential providers.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
When will tutoring begin?
Schools should submit their roster of students who will receive tutoring prior to the start of tutoring (October 1). Tutoring will begin in October 2026.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: When will I know if I’m in the treatment or delayed treatment group?
text:
When will I know if I’m in the treatment or delayed treatment group?
Schools will learn of their treatment status by September 1, 2026 at the latest. Remember: All schools will receive tutoring and IST support. Cohort 1 treatment schools receive IST support in 2026-2027, and Cohort 1 delayed treatment schools will receive IST support in 2027-2028.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
When will surveys be administered?
Surveys will be administered in October–December and April–May of each academic year, starting in the fall of 2026.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: How many students will receive tutoring?
text:
How many students will receive tutoring?
A minimum of 50 students will receive tutoring at each school, though some schools may choose to fund tutoring for more students as needed.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: When will interviews and focus groups take place?
text:
When will interviews and focus groups take place?
Tutor and school staff interviews, as well as family focus groups, will take place in April or May, starting in 2027.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: What incentives will be offered to interview participants?
text:
What incentives will be offered to interview participants?
Unless prohibited by your LEA, all interview and focus group participants will receive a $25 gift card, delivered electronically after the conversation.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
What is a Design Sprint?
A Design Sprint is a multi-week (4), cohort-based program for school teams to shift mindsets and build technical skills to launch and support higher-quality, higher-impact tutoring interventions. Design Sprints will likely be in person, but could be virtual or hybrid depending on LEA preference and scheduling. Design Sprint topics include:

Session 1: Grounding in Equity and Aligning on Tutoring
Session 2: Clarifying the Problem Through Data
Session 3: Gathering Empathy and Insight
Session 4: Designing for Impact

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: Who is expected to participate in the Design Sprint from my school?
text:
Who is expected to participate in the Design Sprint from my school?
3–4 individuals from each school typically attend. The group should include:

A decision maker (typically the principal)
Someone who understands the school’s ELA academic program (usually an assistant principal or curriculum coach)
Someone who understands the school’s schedule and/or roster (usually an assistant principal or counselor)

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: What will the Communities of Practice include?
text:
What will the Communities of Practice include?
Within the CoPs, schools will gather to reflect on data, tackle common challenges, and make plans to strengthen implementation for the second half of the year.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: When will the Communities of Practice be held?
text:
When will the Communities of Practice be held?
CoP #1 will be in January 2027. CoP #2 will be in March–April 2027.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: When will coaching check-ins be held?
text:
When will coaching check-ins be held?
Check-in #1 will be in December 2026. Check-in #2 will be in February–March 2027. Check-in #3 will be in May 2027.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: Will CoPs and coaching be in-person or virtual?
text:
Will CoPs and coaching be in-person or virtual?
Both CoPs and coaching check-ins will be held virtually.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text:
When will observations occur?
CSC will observe tutoring twice throughout the year: in November–December 2026 and March–April 2027.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: What tutoring providers will CSC partner with?
text:
What tutoring providers will CSC partner with?
CSC plans to partner with multiple tutoring providers. Providers include Reading Partners, Reading Assist, and the University of Maryland.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: Was this study reviewed by an IRB?
text:
Was this study reviewed by an IRB?
Yes. This study was reviewed by Sterling IRB (IRB ID: 16024-DTGibney) and received the Exempt determination for a Category 1 Exemption (Research conducted in established or commonly accepted educational settings, involving normal educational practices).

title: Frequently Asked Questions
section: I have more questions. Who can I contact?
text:
I have more questions. Who can I contact?
Reach out to CSCstudy@bellwether.org. Bellwether will either answer the question or pass it along to CSC to answer, if appropriate.

title: About Bellwether
text:
Bellwether is a national nonprofit organization that works to transform education for underserved students. Through research, policy analysis, and advisory work, Bellwether helps education organizations of all kinds improve their effectiveness and drive meaningful change for students.
For the IST study, Bellwether serves as the independent evaluator, meaning Bellwether’s team conducts the research on tutoring outcomes and implementation separately from CSC’s program team. This independence is essential to producing credible findings.
Read more about Bellwether at bellwether.org.

title: About Bellwether
text:
Bellwether is a national nonprofit organization that works to transform education for underserved students. Through research, policy analysis, and advisory work, Bellwether helps education organizations of all kinds improve their effectiveness and drive meaningful change for students.
For the IST study, Bellwether serves as the independent evaluator, meaning Bellwether’s team conducts the research on tutoring outcomes and implementation separately from CSC’s program team. This independence is essential to producing credible findings.
Read more about Bellwether at bellwether.org.

title: About Bellwether
text:
Bellwether’s Related Work
With experience manipulating existing datasets, designing instruments, collecting original data, and executing various qualitative and quantitative analytic methods, the Bellwether evaluation team has served a variety of education agencies and public school clients. It has deep experience designing and conducting impact studies designed to meet WWC standards, including serving as the external evaluator for three Charter Schools Program Replication and Expansion Grants, two Statewide Family Engagement Center Grants, a Full Service Community Schools Grant, and two Teacher Quality Program Grants that all employ quasi-experimental methods to assess program impact.
Bellwether also has expertise in tutoring and tutoring design, having created the Tutoring Quality Improvement System in collaboration with Stanford University to help districts and schools assess the quality of their tutoring. Read more here.

title: About CitySchools Collaborative (CSC)
text: CitySchools Collaborative (CSC) is a nonprofit organization dedicated to expanding access to high-quality, high-impact tutoring for students who need it most. Based in Washington, DC, CSC works directly with schools and districts to build the planning infrastructure, coaching support, and quality monitoring systems that allow tutoring programs to succeed. CSC is DC’s trusted partner in citywide initiatives related to tutoring, talent, and truancy, having served over 100 schools since 2021.

title: About CitySchools Collaborative (CSC)
text: CitySchools Collaborative (CSC) is a nonprofit organization dedicated to expanding access to high-quality, high-impact tutoring for students who need it most. Based in Washington, DC, CSC works directly with schools and districts to build the planning infrastructure, coaching support, and quality monitoring systems that allow tutoring programs to succeed. CSC is DC’s trusted partner in citywide initiatives related to tutoring, talent, and truancy, having served over 100 schools since 2021.

title: About CitySchools Collaborative (CSC)
text:
Our Approach
CSC’s model goes beyond placing tutors in classrooms. We partner with schools to:

Align tutoring to the Science of Reading and to classroom instruction
Build consistent, protected time for tutoring into the school day
Monitor implementation to catch problems early
Use data to track progress and inform decision-making

This comprehensive approach is what the IST study is designed to evaluate, and what CSC believes makes high-impact tutoring truly high-impact.
Read more about us at cityschoolscollab.org.

“The single program that has made the biggest difference in Truth’s improved academic outcomes has been High-Impact Tutoring…It has made a critical difference for our students who have been struggling with math through these post-pandemic years.”
— Justin Lessek, Executive Director, Sojourner Truth PCS


“I feel very confident about learning new things with my tutor when I don’t understand my work.”
— Tutored Student, Middle School

title: About CitySchools Collaborative (CSC)
text:
Proven Results
A previous study of CSC’s tutoring program found that students who received high-intensity math tutoring from CSC providers in 2023–2025 received the equivalent of an additional 59 instructional days and achieved, on average, 138% of their expected growth on math assessments.
→ Read the QED Report

title: About CitySchools Collaborative (CSC)
text:
Who CSC Has Served
CSC has worked with over 100 schools since 2020. Below are a few LEAs CSC has worked with in the District of Columbia:





















Read more about how CSC has impacted schools in their annual impact reports.
Read more about how CSC has impacted these schools in their annual impact reports.

title: For Parents and Families
text:
If your student’s school participates in this program, your student may be selected to receive free literacy tutoring during the school day. Here’s what that looks like:

Tutoring happens during school hours: no after-school pickup or additional scheduling required for families
Sessions are small-group or one-to-one: your student gets personalized help where they need it the most
Tutoring is aligned to the Science of Reading: tutoring curriculum is research-backed and builds on what your child is learning in class
There is no cost to families: all tutoring costs are covered by CSC and your student’s school

title: For Parents and Families
text:
If your student’s school participates in this program, your student may be selected to receive free literacy tutoring during the school day. Here’s what that looks like:

Tutoring happens during school hours: no after-school pickup or additional scheduling required for families
Sessions are small-group or one-to-one: your student gets personalized help where they need it the most
Tutoring is aligned to the Science of Reading: tutoring curriculum is research-backed and builds on what your child is learning in class
There is no cost to families: all tutoring costs are covered by CSC and your student’s school

title: For Parents and Families
section: Will my student’s information be shared?
text:
Will my student’s information be shared?
Student privacy is a top priority. Any information collected as part of the research study follows federal privacy laws and district/state data agreements. Results from the study will be reported in aggregate, meaning no individual student or school is identified in any published findings. Additionally, all data for the study will remain confidential and secure in each of the following ways:

All survey data will be made unidentifiable before being sent to Bellwether.
All student academic test scores and program data will be stored in a secure, access-controlled environment accessible only to authorized Bellwether research personnel.
All data will be shared directly into secure folders or via encrypted transfer.
Bellwether will comply with FERPA, applicable state privacy laws, and all data security provisions in the data sharing agreement made with each school.
All data will be destroyed or returned to each data sharing partner when the study is over.

title: For Parents and Families
section: Can I opt my student out of the study?
text:
Can I opt my student out of the study?
If your student has been selected for tutoring, your school will notify you and provide a Parent/Guardian Consent Form to ask whether you allow your student to participate in the study aspect of the program. If your student receives tutoring, please fill out the Parent/Guardian Consent Form and return it to your school, even if you do not want your student to participate in the study. Having the “do not consent” on file will help ensure that schools share data only from students whose parents or guardians have consented to the study. Your student will still receive tutoring, even if you do not consent to them participating in the study.

title: For Parents and Families
text:
Have more questions?
Contact Us →

title: How to Get Involved
text:
Participating schools commit to a focused set of actions that CSC supports throughout:

Designate a point of contact: One staff member (often an assistant principal, literacy coach, or instructional coordinator) serves as the primary liaison with CSC staff.
Sign a Data Sharing Agreement (DSA): A standard agreement that allows CSC and researchers to access existing student roster and assessment data. This follows all applicable federal and state privacy laws.
Identify 50 or more eligible students: Work with CSC to determine which students in grades 4–8 would benefit most from literacy tutoring.
Schedule tutoring time during the Design Sprint: Carve out approximately 90 minutes per week of tutoring time during the school day, for a minimum of 10 weeks. CSC facilitates and supports this planning.
Share a student roster: Before tutoring begins, share the list of all students eligible for tutoring.
Implement HIT: Implement 90 minutes a week of tutoring for at least 10 weeks for identified students.
Allow observations: Allow CSC to monitor tutoring via two observations a year.
Share interim assessment data: If your LEA cannot share interim literacy assessment data (NWEA MAP, i-Ready, etc.), schools will be expected to share participating students’ interim assessment data at the end of the school year.
Encourage staff, students, and families to complete surveys: Annual feedback surveys help CSC continuously improve its programming.
Participate in brief research activities: Some schools may be asked to participate in site visits or interviews with staff, families, and leaders to help researchers understand how implementation is working.

CSC staff and the Bellwether research team work with your team throughout this process.

title: How to Get Involved
text:
Ready to Get Started?
Sign Up to Participate → Contact Us with Questions →

title: Resources
text:
High-impact tutoring (HIT) refers to tutoring that meets specific quality thresholds that research has found to produce meaningful gains in student learning. Key features of high-impact tutoring include:

Frequency and dosage: Sessions occur at least three times per week, for 30 minutes or more per week, for at least 10 weeks
Small groups or one-to-one: Tutors work with no more than four students at a time
High-quality, Science-of-Reading–aligned curriculum materials

Research consistently finds that high-impact tutoring can produce significant gains for struggling students, particularly in reading and math. The IST study focuses specifically on literacy tutoring for students in grades 4–8.
Learn more about the benefits of high-impact tutoring:

Tutoring quality standards
HIT tutoring toolkit
Research briefs on HIT

title: Resources
text:
Previous CSC Study Report
A previous study of CSC’s tutoring program found that students who received high-intensity math tutoring from CSC providers in 2023–2025 received the equivalent of an additional 59 instructional days and achieved, on average, 138% of their expected growth on math assessments.
→ Read the QED Report

title: Resources
text:
About the Science of Reading
The Science of Reading is a decades-long body of research from cognitive science, linguistics, and education that explains how reading develops. It emphasizes systematic, explicit instruction in the five key areas identified by the National Reading Panel: phonemic awareness, phonics, fluency, vocabulary, and comprehension.
CSC’s tutoring program is designed to be fully aligned with Science of Reading principles, ensuring students receive instruction that is grounded in what the research shows works.
Learn more:

Defining the Science of Reading
In-depth guide to the Science of Reading
Resources supported by the Maryland State Education Association

title: The Intermediary Support for Tutoring (IST) Study
text:
Reading proficiency in the United States remains a persistent challenge. High-impact tutoring (HIT) has emerged as one of the strongest evidence-based strategies to address this gap. HIT is high-dosage tutoring delivered either one-on-one or in small groups, using high-quality curriculum and materials. It emphasizes frequent, personalized interactions with students and alignment with students’ regular classroom learning. See the Resources tab for additional information and reading on HIT.
But tutoring alone is not enough. The way tutoring is organized, scheduled, aligned to instruction, and monitored might impact how much students get out of their time with tutors.
The IST study is designed to test whether CSC’s IST support model helps make tutoring more effective for students. By studying this rigorously, CSC and its research partner, Bellwether, aim to generate evidence to guide schools, districts, and policymakers across the country in planning and investing in tutoring programs.

title: The Intermediary Support for Tutoring (IST) Study
text:
Reading proficiency in the United States remains a persistent challenge. High-impact tutoring (HIT) has emerged as one of the strongest evidence-based strategies to address this gap. HIT is high-dosage tutoring delivered either one-on-one or in small groups, using high-quality curriculum and materials. It emphasizes frequent, personalized interactions with students and alignment with students’ regular classroom learning. See the Resources tab for additional information and reading on HIT.
But tutoring alone is not enough. The way tutoring is organized, scheduled, aligned to instruction, and monitored might impact how much students get out of their time with tutors.
The IST study is designed to test whether CSC’s IST support model helps make tutoring more effective for students. By studying this rigorously, CSC and its research partner, Bellwether, aim to generate evidence to guide schools, districts, and policymakers across the country in planning and investing in tutoring programs.

title: The Intermediary Support for Tutoring (IST) Study
text:
How the Study Works
The IST study uses a randomized design, which is the gold standard for understanding whether a program works. It will include three cohorts of schools, with Cohort 1 starting in school year 2026-27. Schools that agree to participate in Cohort 1 will be randomly assigned to one of two groups:

Group 1 (treatment): Schools receive subsidized literacy tutoring and CSC’s full implementation support, including structured planning, coaching, and data tools beginning in the upcoming 2026–27 school year.
Group 2 (delayed treatment): Schools receive access to subsidized literacy tutoring from the start, but receive CSC’s full implementation support in a later year (2027–28). These schools still benefit from participation and will receive a full two years of subsidized tutoring.

This design allows researchers to compare outcomes between schools with and without full implementation support while ensuring that all schools receive the same benefits.
The study will include two more cohorts of schools in school years 2027-28 and 2028-29 that will also be randomly assigned to treatment and delayed treatment.

title: The Intermediary Support for Tutoring (IST) Study
text:
Who Is Funding This Study?
The IST study is funded by the U.S. Department of Education through a competitive Education Innovation and Research (EIR) grant. The project runs from October 1, 2025 through September 30, 2030, with a total federal investment of $10 million. EIR grants provide funding to help create or expand promising practices that have the potential to address persistent challenges in education. Read more about EIR grants here.

title: The Intermediary Support for Tutoring (IST) Study
text:
Who Is Leading the Study?





CitySchools Collaborative (CSC) leads program implementation, including recruiting schools, delivering tutoring, and providing the implementation support that is being studied.





Bellwether leads the independent evaluation.
